paragraphs.speaker: guy
paragraphs.name: Guy Raz
paragraphs.text: Yvon Chouinard isn't the kind of entrepreneur who's going to wow you with a PowerPoint presentation or a snappy elevator pitch. He's basically a surfer, a kayaker, and a mountain climber who sort of stumbled into the role of businessman. Yvon's never had any interest in becoming rich. He's actively worked to slow down Patagonia's growth and profit margins. And yet, despite his best efforts to keep it small, this little clothing company he started 50 years ago has turned into a huge business.

paragraphs.speaker: yvon
paragraphs.name: Yvon Chouinard
paragraphs.text: I never intended to start a business. I just have this knack that every time I look at a product, I think, I can make something better than that. Every time we went climbing, we'd come back with ideas on how to improve the climbing gear, which was very crude in those days. So I became a blacksmith. I taught myself blacksmithing so that I could make some of the climbing hardware that I thought would be better than what's available.

paragraphs.speaker: guy
paragraphs.name: Guy Raz
paragraphs.text: You were literally like pounding hot pieces of metal into shape?

paragraphs.speaker: yvon
paragraphs.name: Yvon Chouinard
paragraphs.text: That's exactly right. Doing it in my parents' backyard. And then my father helped me convert a chicken coop into a little shop. I was making pitons, those metal spikes that you pound into cracks. And I was making the carabiners, the snap links and hard goods like that.

paragraphs.speaker: yvon
paragraphs.name: Yvon Chouinard
paragraphs.text: There were probably 250 climbers in the country. All the gear came from Europe and it was pretty poor quality. The pitons were made to be used once because the Europeans looked at climbing as conquering mountains. We were brought up kind of the philosophy of John Muir and Thoreau. We felt like nature should be left alone and you should do a climb but leave nothing behind. So I made pitons out of better materials that could be put in and taken out and used over and over again. The European pitons were selling for 15 cents a piece. And mine were a dollar and a half.

paragraphs.speaker: yvon
paragraphs.name: Yvon Chouinard
paragraphs.text: I was on a climbing trip to Scotland in the winter. I was walking by some shop and I saw a rugby shirt in the window. And I thought, wow, that's pretty cool. It's real colorful. It had blue and red and yellow and made really tough. So I thought, let's make a really great shirt for climbing. The collar kind of keeps the gear slings from cutting into your neck. At that time, active sportswear for men was basically gray sweatshirts and sweatpants. Men did not wear anything colorful. So I started wearing this climbing and everybody was going, wow, where'd you get that? I thought, well, maybe I ought to import a few of these from England and see if they'll sell. And sure enough, they sold. And I realized it's a lot more profitable than making pitons.

paragraphs.speaker: yvon
paragraphs.name: Yvon Chouinard
paragraphs.text: In 68, I did a six month long trip from California down to South America. We drove and surfed all the way down to coasts, down to Lima, and then sold the surfboards and then climbed volcanoes down in southern Chile and skied off of them. I kind of fell in love with that area of Patagonia. In those days, nobody even knew what Patagonia was. I decided to call it Patagonia because I wanted to make clothing for those kind of conditions, you know, Cape Horn. Super high winds, cold, crazy temperatures, change in weather.

paragraphs.speaker: yvon
paragraphs.name: Yvon Chouinard
paragraphs.text: One of the first products we made was a pair of climbing shorts and I made the pattern for it. I knew nothing about pattern making. I just took a pair of shorts apart and looked at it. I wanted to make a really tough pair of shorts. So I used canvas from outdoor furniture or something. I had this Korean woman working for me and she had to sew it on a walking foot machine, which is the kind of sewing machine that you use for sewing leather because the fabric was so thick. When she was done with them and she stood them up, they stood straight up. So we named them stand-up shorts. It took about 50 washings to break them in, but we still make those shorts.

paragraphs.speaker: yvon
paragraphs.name: Yvon Chouinard
paragraphs.text: We were so successful in the late 80s that we were growing too fast. We were growing 50 percent a year. And you can't do that for very long before you run out of money. We were all ramped up to grow 50 percent and we only grew 25, which doesn't sound bad. But when you build all the inventory for that and you don't make it, it's pretty rough.
